subject.description: Chaucer's five-book Middle English prose translation

reviewNotes: Boethius is author of the Latin source work; Chaucer is translator and adapter of the Middle English Boece; Morris is editor of the immediate edition.
The edition has a base-witness-plus-collation lineage, not a single-witness lineage.
The local 1999 availability notice and U-M's current No Copyright–United States statement are preserved as separate provider statements.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy or version of U-M CME item ChaucerBo, keyed from Richard Morris's 1868 EETS edition of Geoffrey Chaucer's Middle English prose translation of Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy. Morris edited British Library Add MS 10340 as his base and collated Cambridge University Library MS Ii.3.21; bracketed whole words generally come from the Cambridge witness. The XML also includes Morris's introduction and appendices but omits the printed synoptic marginal gloss, most textual footnotes, and glossarial index.

catalogSummary.author: Anicius Manlius Severinus Boethius; translated and adapted by Geoffrey Chaucer
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of translation not securely established
catalogSummary.title: Boece

catalogingSubject.label: Boece
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's five-book Middle English prose translation and adaptation of Boethius's De consolatione philosophiae, as represented by the text in Morris's 1868 edition and U-M CME item ChaucerBo. Morris's introduction, comparison extracts, table, appendix, index, and appended poems are edition components rather than part of the cataloged medieval work.

contentParts.label: Book I
contentParts.summary: Boethius laments his fall; Lady Philosophy diagnoses his grief and begins to restore his understanding.

contentParts.label: Book II
contentParts.summary: Philosophy exposes Fortune's mutability and the inadequacy of wealth, office, fame, and bodily pleasure.

contentParts.label: Book III
contentParts.summary: The dialogue identifies the supreme good, true happiness, unity, and God as the end sought through imperfect goods.

contentParts.label: Book IV
contentParts.summary: Philosophy considers evil, punishment, providence, fate, and the apparent success of the wicked.

contentParts.label: Book V
contentParts.summary: The final book reconciles chance, free will, divine foreknowledge, and eternity.

notes: The normalized range expresses uncertainty; it is not a claim for exact beginning and ending years.

dateStatements.display: British Library Add MS 10340, first quarter of the fifteenth century
notes: This is a date for an extant manuscript copy, not for composition.

extent.description: Five books comprising 40 prose divisions and 39 divisions corresponding to Boethius's metres, all rendered by Chaucer in Middle English prose. Counts exclude Morris's introduction, index, table, appendix, and appended comparative material.

formStatements.description: Chaucer renders both Boethius's prose sections and Latin metres in Middle English prose; XML unit labels ‘prose’ and ‘metre’ identify source divisions rather than alternating Middle English forms.

scope.description: Latin lemmata, headings, and source phrases occur within the Middle English work

notes: The work-level form is prose even though its source is a prosimetrum and the XML labels 39 divisions ‘metre’; Chaucer translates those metres into prose.
French and Italian in the XML language profile occur in Morris's editorial quotations and do not make the medieval work quadrilingual.
The edition's base manuscript and collated witness are recorded separately so supplied Cambridge readings are not mistaken for Add MS 10340 readings.

next_steps: Consult current Boece chronology scholarship and the full dating argument in Sources of the Boece.
openQuestions.question: Can the date or phases of Chaucer's translation be narrowed beyond ‘around 1380’ without overstating disputed evidence?

next_steps: Compare Morris's full apparatus and bracket conventions with both manuscripts; do not treat every bracket as the same kind of intervention.
openQuestions.question: Which individual electronic readings derive from CUL Ii.3.21 rather than Morris's Add MS 10340 base?

notes: No exact place of translation and no single scribal dialect region are asserted.
Recorded as source-work authorship; Chaucer is responsible for the Middle English translation.

scope.description: Authorship of the Latin source work translated in Boece
Prose reworking drawing on Latin, French, and commentary traditions
Edited from British Library Add MS 10340
Collated against Cambridge University Library MS Ii.3.21

summaries.value: Chaucer's Middle English prose translation and adaptation of Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy presents the imprisoned Boethius's dialogue with Lady Philosophy. Across five books it moves from grief and Fortune's instability through the nature of happiness and goodness to providence, fate, foreknowledge, and human freedom.

The XML package contains extensive nineteenth-century editorial matter in addition to Boece. This metadata record treats Chaucer's five-book translation as its intellectual subject and records Morris's framing materials only to prevent their accidental attribution to Chaucer.
